subtopic_detail: B12 deficiency with neurological involvement parenteral hydroxocobalamin
stem: You are the general medical registrar reviewing a 70-year-old woman with 4 months of progressive lower-limb paraesthesias, an unsteady gait, mild memory disturbance, and exertional dyspnoea. She has no history of bowel surgery, has had a long-standing diagnosis of atrophic gastritis, has never been a vegetarian, and is otherwise self-caring at home. She has not been on metformin or proton pump inhibitor therapy chronically. On examination she has reduced vibration and proprioception in both lower limbs with brisk knee reflexes and absent ankle reflexes, and a positive Romberg test. She has no jaundice, no glossitis on examination today, and no pancytopenia.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 88, BP 122/74, RR 16, SpO2 97% room air
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, no jaundice, no glossitis, neurology with reduced vibration and proprioception lower limbs bilaterally, absent ankle reflexes, brisk knee reflexes, positive Romberg, no focal cortical deficit
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 normocytic transitioning to macrocytic with MCV 108, WCC 4.2, platelets 220, B12 95 (low), serum folate 9 (normal), iron studies normal, TSH normal, anti-intrinsic factor antibody positive, MMA elevated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence intramuscular hydroxocobalamin 1 mg every second day for 2 weeks then 1 mg every 3 months long-term, monitor neurological recovery and full blood examination at 6 weeks, and screen for autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes given the intrinsic factor antibodies
options.rationale: B12 deficiency with neurological involvement is treated with parenteral hydroxocobalamin loading every second day for 1-2 weeks then 3-monthly maintenance; oral B12 is inadequate when intrinsic factor antibodies are present. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency

options.text: Commence oral cyanocobalamin 1 mg daily for 4 months as monotherapy regardless of underlying intrinsic factor antibody positivity, with planned full blood examination and neurological reassessment at week 6 to confirm a satisfactory clinical response
options.rationale: Oral B12 1 mg daily can work in dietary deficiency but is unreliable when intrinsic factor antibodies are present; parenteral therapy is preferred in pernicious anaemia, particularly with neurological involvement. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency

options.text: Commence oral folic acid 5 mg daily as initial therapy with planned repeat B12 in 6 weeks to assess response, with parenteral B12 added only if anaemia does not improve adequately on folate monotherapy at the planned 6-week review
options.rationale: Folic acid alone can precipitate or worsen subacute combined degeneration in B12 deficiency and is contraindicated as monotherapy; B12 must be replaced first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency

options.text: Refer urgently to haematology for bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy to characterise the macrocytic anaemia and rule out myelodysplastic syndrome before commencing any B12 replacement therapy, with follow-up neurology assessment within 2 weeks
options.rationale: Bone marrow biopsy is not needed when serum B12 is low with positive intrinsic factor antibodies; diagnosis is established and treatment should commence promptly. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency

options.text: Arrange transfusion of two units of packed red cells now to correct the anaemia and improve oxygen delivery, with parenteral B12 commenced after the transfusion is complete and the haemoglobin has stabilised above the threshold of 100 g/L
options.rationale: Transfusion is reserved for severe symptomatic anaemia or cardiorespiratory compromise; macrocytic anaemia at Hb 96 with B12 deficiency responds to B12 replacement and does not need urgent transfusion. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency

explanation.summary: Vitamin B12 deficiency with neurological involvement requires prompt parenteral replacement to limit the risk of irreversible subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord. Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg intramuscularly every second day for 1 to 2 weeks loads body stores, followed by 1 mg every 3 months for maintenance in pernicious anaemia. Folate must not be given alone in B12 deficiency as it can precipitate neurological deterioration. Pernicious anaemia is associated with autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes, gastric carcinoid tumours, and gastric adenocarcinoma, so surveillance gastroscopy and screening for other autoimmunity (thyroid, adrenal, type 1 diabetes) is offered.
key_points: Parenteral hydroxocobalamin is preferred for neurological involvement or pernicious anaemia
Folate alone is contraindicated as monotherapy in B12 deficiency
Surveillance for autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes and gastric malignancy
explanation.pearls: Methylmalonic acid is the most specific marker for tissue B12 deficiency; homocysteine rises in both B12 and folate deficiency but methylmalonic acid is elevated in B12 deficiency only.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Vitamin B12 Deficiency
BCSH Guideline: Cobalamin and Folate Deficiency

subtopic_detail: Adrenal crisis intravenous hydrocortisone in known Addison's during gastroenteritis
stem: You are the emergency department registrar reviewing a 45-year-old woman with known primary adrenal insufficiency who presents with 36 hours of vomiting, watery diarrhoea, weakness and inability to keep her usual hydrocortisone 20 mg morning, 10 mg afternoon and fludrocortisone 100 micrograms daily down. Her partner brought her in via ambulance after she became drowsy and complained of generalised weakness. She is alert but mildly confused on arrival. She has not received any stress-dose steroid before arrival despite her chronic disease management plan instructing her to double her oral dose during illness and seek urgent review if vomiting prevented oral intake.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.0, HR 122, BP 78/50, RR 22, SpO2 97% room air, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor, hyperpigmented palmar creases and knuckles, mildly tender abdomen without peritonism, no focal neurology, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Na 122, K 6.0, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, urea 14, creatinine 142 (baseline 78), Hb 138, WCC 14.2, lactate 2.6; ECG sinus tachycardia 122; cortisol pre-treatment not yet available

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer oral hydrocortisone 40 mg as a stat dose with antiemetic and oral rehydration solution, with planned review in 2 hours and admission for inpatient observation if there is no improvement in observations and biochemistry at the recheck
options.rationale: Oral hydrocortisone is unreliable in vomiting and hypotensive shock; IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat plus IV fluids is the standard immediate management. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Adrenal Insufficiency)

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat then 50 mg six-hourly, give intravenous normal saline 1 litre stat then ongoing crystalloid resuscitation, treat hypoglycaemia with intravenous 10% dextrose, and admit to high-dependency unit
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is managed with IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat then 50 mg 6-hourly, IV saline for volume resuscitation, IV dextrose for hypoglycaemia, and admission for monitoring; fludrocortisone is not needed acutely as 100 mg IV hydrocortisone provides mineralocorticoid effect. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Adrenal Insufficiency)

options.text: Administer intravenous dexamethasone 4 mg stat as preferred glucocorticoid as it does not interfere with subsequent cortisol assays for the diagnostic short Synacthen test, give intravenous saline, and admit to the medical ward for ongoing observation
options.rationale: Dexamethasone lacks the mineralocorticoid effect needed in adrenal crisis and is not first-line; hydrocortisone is preferred in known Addison's during acute illness. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Adrenal Insufficiency)

options.text: Hold all glucocorticoid until baseline serum cortisol and ACTH results return to confirm the diagnosis of adrenal crisis, give intravenous fluid resuscitation in the interim, with cortisol assays sent before any steroid administration to avoid biochemical interference
options.rationale: Glucocorticoid must not be delayed for confirmatory testing in suspected adrenal crisis; in known Addison's the diagnosis is established and immediate IV hydrocortisone is life-saving. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Adrenal Insufficiency)

options.text: Administer intramuscular fludrocortisone 200 micrograms stat with intravenous saline to address her hyperkalaemia and hyponatraemia, with planned introduction of oral hydrocortisone once she has tolerated oral intake for 4 hours without further vomiting
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone has slow onset and is not for acute adrenal crisis; IV hydrocortisone is the urgent intervention with rapid glucocorticoid and adequate mineralocorticoid effect. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Adrenal Insufficiency)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat followed by 50 mg every 6 hours (or continuous infusion of 200 mg per 24 hours), aggressive intravenous saline resuscitation, intravenous dextrose for hypoglycaemia, and admission for ongoing monitoring. In known Addison's disease, treatment should never be delayed for confirmatory testing. Hydrocortisone at high dose provides sufficient mineralocorticoid effect so fludrocortisone is not added acutely; once stable, fludrocortisone is resumed alongside hydrocortisone wean. Sick-day rules and an emergency hydrocortisone kit at home reduce recurrence.
key_points: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat then 50 mg every 6 hours (or 200 mg/24 h infusion)
IV saline plus IV dextrose if hypoglycaemic
Do not delay treatment for confirmatory testing in known Addison's
explanation.pearls: All patients with primary adrenal insufficiency should carry an emergency hydrocortisone 100 mg vial with needle and syringe, wear a medical alert identifier, and have a written sick-day plan including doubling oral dose for febrile illness.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency Position Statement

subtopic_detail: Type B aortic dissection intravenous labetalol target heart rate and blood pressure
stem: You are the emergency department registrar managing a 60-year-old hypertensive man with sudden onset severe tearing chest and interscapular pain 1 hour ago. He has poorly controlled hypertension on irbesartan 300 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily. He has had no syncope, no neurology deficit, no abdominal pain, and his ECG shows sinus rhythm with left ventricular hypertrophy but no ischaemic features. CT aortogram has demonstrated a Stanford type B dissection commencing distal to the left subclavian artery, descending to the level of the renal arteries with patent visceral vessels and no malperfusion. He has been transferred to the resuscitation cubicle with cardiothoracic surgery and intensive care on standby.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 102, BP 186/108 right arm, 124/78 left arm, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Distressed with pain, diaphoretic, no focal neurology, no abdominal tenderness, peripheral pulses present and symmetrical in lower limbs, no clinical features of cardiac tamponade
data_table.Investigations: Trop normal, FBC, UEC, coags normal, ECG sinus tachycardia 102, CXR mediastinum slightly widened, CT aortogram: Stanford type B dissection commencing distal to left subclavian and extending to renal artery level, no end-organ malperfusion, true lumen patent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate medical management?

options.text: Commence oral irbesartan 600 mg as an additional dose plus oral nifedipine modified-release 60 mg orally for blood pressure reduction, with admission to the coronary care unit for cardiac monitoring and serial troponin assessment over the next 24 hours
options.rationale: Oral agents have slow onset and unpredictable effect in dissection; IV beta-blocker (labetalol or esmolol) is first-line for acute heart rate and blood pressure control. (Source: CSANZ: Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Administer intravenous adenosine 6 mg rapid push to slow the heart rate to a target less than 60 beats per minute and reduce shear stress on the dissection flap, with continuous cardiac monitoring and repeat doses titrated to clinical response
options.rationale: Adenosine is for paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, not for chronic rate control; labetalol or esmolol infusion is the appropriate agent. (Source: CSANZ: Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Commence intravenous labetalol infusion targeting a heart rate below 60 beats per minute first, then add sodium nitroprusside infusion if systolic blood pressure remains above 120 mmHg, target SBP 100-120 mmHg, opioid for pain, ICU admission
options.rationale: Anti-impulse therapy with IV beta-blocker (target HR less than 60) precedes vasodilator (nitroprusside or GTN) to avoid reflex tachycardia and shear stress; SBP target 100-120; uncomplicated type B is medically managed initially. (Source: CSANZ: Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Proceed urgently to operating theatre for open surgical repair of the descending aorta given the severity of dissection and high in-hospital mortality of medically managed type B dissection presentations, with cardiopulmonary bypass team standby for the procedure
options.rationale: Uncomplicated type B dissection is managed medically; surgical or endovascular intervention is reserved for malperfusion, rupture, or refractory symptoms. (Source: CSANZ: Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Commence intravenous heparin infusion at 18 units per kilogram per hour to prevent thrombosis within the false lumen and stabilise the dissection flap, with continuous cardiac and APTT monitoring during heparinisation in the intensive care unit
options.rationale: Anticoagulation is contraindicated in acute aortic dissection due to risk of haemorrhage and aortic rupture; rate and BP control are the priorities. (Source: CSANZ: Aortic Dissection)

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated type B aortic dissection is medically managed with anti-impulse therapy to reduce aortic wall shear stress. IV beta-blocker (labetalol or esmolol infusion) is given first to target heart rate below 60 beats per minute, then a vasodilator (sodium nitroprusside or glyceryl trinitrate) is added to target systolic blood pressure 100 to 120 mmHg. Beta-blocker must precede vasodilator to avoid reflex tachycardia. Pain is controlled with opioid. ICU admission for monitoring and cardiothoracic input. Complicated type B (malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain or expansion) requires thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) or open surgery.
key_points: Beta-blocker first (HR target less than 60), then vasodilator (SBP 100-120)
Uncomplicated type B: medical management; type A: emergency surgical repair
explanation.pearls: Pulse and blood pressure should be measured in both arms in suspected dissection; a difference greater than 20 mmHg in systolic pressure is a classic but non-sensitive sign and absence does not exclude dissection.

subtopic_detail: Phaeochromocytoma alpha-blockade prior to beta-blockade before adrenalectomy
stem: You are the endocrinology registrar reviewing a 50-year-old man newly diagnosed with phaeochromocytoma after presenting with paroxysmal headaches, sweating and palpitations, with episodic systolic blood pressures up to 220 mmHg recorded by his general practitioner. Twenty-four-hour fractionated urinary metanephrines and plasma free metanephrines have confirmed the diagnosis. A 5 cm right adrenal mass has been imaged on contrast computed tomography of the abdomen, with no metastatic disease on the staging scans. He has been referred for elective adrenalectomy and the surgical team has asked for preoperative medical optimisation. He is otherwise well, on no current medications, and has no other comorbidities.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/96 resting (220 systolic during paroxysms), HR 96, BMI 25
data_table.Examination: Mildly tremulous, no thyroid enlargement, normal cardiovascular examination at rest, no abdominal mass palpable
data_table.Investigations: Plasma free metanephrines and normetanephrines markedly raised, 24-hour urinary metanephrines raised, CT abdomen 5 cm right adrenal mass with classic phaeochromocytoma features and no metastases, MIBG and PET pending; ECG sinus rhythm, normal echocardiogram, normal renal function

lead_in: What is the most appropriate preoperative medical preparation?

options.text: Commence atenolol 50 mg daily for 7 days as initial preoperative beta-blockade before introducing alpha-blockade with phenoxybenzamine, to achieve dual receptor control before the planned surgical excision and avoid intraoperative haemodynamic instability
options.rationale: Beta-blockade before alpha-blockade in phaeochromocytoma causes unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction and hypertensive crisis; alpha-blockade must be commenced first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Proceed directly to adrenalectomy without preoperative medical preparation given the convenience of intraoperative haemodynamic management by experienced anaesthetics and surgical teams, with intravenous phentolamine and sodium nitroprusside available at induction
options.rationale: Preoperative alpha-blockade for 10-14 days reduces mortality from haemodynamic instability during surgery; foregoing preparation is standard of care violation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Commence amlodipine 5 mg daily titrated to blood pressure control as the primary preoperative agent and salt and fluid replacement to optimise intravascular volume, with planned surgery in 2 weeks once normotension is achieved on this regimen
options.rationale: Calcium channel blockers are second-line adjuncts in phaeochromocytoma; alpha-blockade is mainstay of preoperative preparation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Commence phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily, titrate every 2 to 3 days targeting symptom control and supine BP less than 130/80 with orthostatic systolic above 90, encourage high-salt diet and oral fluids, add a beta-blocker only after established alpha-blockade for residual tachycardia
options.rationale: Phenoxybenzamine for 10-14 days with up-titration, salt and fluid loading, and addition of beta-blocker only after alpha-blockade is established, is standard preoperative preparation for phaeochromocytoma. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Commence metyrosine 250 mg four times daily for catecholamine synthesis inhibition for 7 days preoperatively, with planned introduction of carvedilol after the initial week of metyrosine to provide concurrent alpha and beta receptor antagonism prior to surgery
options.rationale: Metyrosine is reserved for metastatic phaeochromocytoma or refractory cases; not first-line preoperative therapy for surgical candidates. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology: Phaeochromocytoma)

explanation.summary: Preoperative preparation for phaeochromocytoma comprises alpha-adrenergic blockade for 10 to 14 days, traditionally with phenoxybenzamine (non-selective, irreversible) starting at 10 mg twice daily and titrated up to symptom control and target blood pressure less than 130/80 supine with mild orthostatic hypotension. High-salt diet and oral fluid loading reverse the chronically constricted intravascular volume. Beta-blocker is added only after established alpha-blockade to control reflex tachycardia; reversing this order causes unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction and hypertensive crisis. Doxazosin (selective alpha-1) is an alternative to phenoxybenzamine.
key_points: Alpha-blockade for 10-14 days before beta-blocker addition
Phenoxybenzamine titrated to BP under 130/80 supine plus orthostasis
High-salt diet and fluid loading reverse chronic vasoconstriction
explanation.pearls: Adequacy of preoperative alpha-blockade is judged by Roizen criteria: BP less than 160/90 for 24 hours, no orthostatic hypotension below 80/45, ECG free of ST-T changes for 1 week, and no more than 5 ectopics per minute.

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma

subtopic_detail: Clinically isolated syndrome optic neuritis MRI brain and spine for MS workup
stem: You are the neurology registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman with 5 days of progressive unilateral painful vision loss in the right eye, worsened by eye movement, with reduced colour vision and a relative afferent pupillary defect on examination. She has had no previous neurological symptoms, no recent infection, no headache outside of the painful eye movements, and is otherwise systemically well. Fundoscopy shows a mildly swollen optic disc. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension and giant cell arteritis have been excluded clinically. There are no features of inflammatory eye disease, and she has not been exposed to ethambutol, methanol or other optic neurotoxins recently.

data_table.Examination: Right visual acuity 6/36, left 6/6; reduced colour vision right eye; relative afferent pupillary defect right; mild disc swelling right; full extraocular movements with pain on right eye motion; no other cranial nerve deficit; normal upper and lower limb power, tone and reflexes; no sensory deficit
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, ESR, CRP and B12 normal, syphilis serology negative, ANA negative; ECG normal; visual evoked potentials pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation and management plan?

options.text: Commence intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days empirically for likely optic neuritis with planned outpatient oral prednisolone taper over 3 weeks, with magnetic resonance imaging deferred to outpatient review in 2 weeks once acute treatment is complete
options.rationale: MRI brain and spine should be performed prior to or concurrent with treatment to assess dissemination of demyelinating lesions for MS diagnosis; oral taper after IV steroid is not standard. (Source: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines)
source_refs: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines

options.text: Arrange urgent ocular ultrasound and orbital CT to characterise optic disc oedema and any associated mass lesion compressing the optic nerve, with planned ophthalmology review the next day for visual evoked potentials and consideration of intervention
options.rationale: Ocular ultrasound and CT are not the right imaging for optic neuritis or MS workup; MRI brain and spine with gadolinium is the test of choice. (Source: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines)
source_refs: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines

options.text: Reassure the patient that idiopathic optic neuritis usually resolves spontaneously over 2 to 4 weeks without intervention, advise outpatient ophthalmology review in 1 week and request visual evoked potentials testing without further imaging or systemic therapy
options.rationale: Optic neuritis is the most common first presentation of multiple sclerosis and MRI is required to assess dissemination; treatment with IV steroids may shorten recovery. (Source: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines)
source_refs: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines

options.text: Arrange urgent contrast magnetic resonance imaging of the brain and whole spinal cord to assess for additional demyelinating lesions, refer to neurology and ophthalmology, consider intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 to 5 days to hasten visual recovery
options.rationale: Optic neuritis suspected as a clinically isolated syndrome requires MRI brain and spine with gadolinium for McDonald criteria assessment, neurology referral, and consideration of IV methylprednisolone (Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial) to hasten recovery. (Source: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines)
source_refs: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines

options.text: Commence empirical ocrelizumab 600 mg intravenously now as disease-modifying therapy for presumed relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, with planned outpatient ophthalmology follow-up in 1 week to monitor visual recovery and treatment tolerability
options.rationale: Disease-modifying therapy is commenced after diagnosis of MS confirmed by clinical and radiological criteria, not for an undiagnosed first event without imaging confirmation. (Source: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines)
source_refs: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines

explanation.summary: Acute optic neuritis is the most common first manifestation of multiple sclerosis. Diagnostic workup requires contrast magnetic resonance imaging of the brain and whole spinal cord to look for additional demyelinating lesions satisfying McDonald criteria for dissemination in space and time. Intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 to 5 days hastens visual recovery without changing long-term visual outcome (Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial); oral prednisolone alone is associated with worse outcomes and is not recommended. Disease-modifying therapy is initiated after confirmation of MS diagnosis, with selection guided by disease severity and patient factors.
key_points: MRI brain and spine with gadolinium is the imaging modality of choice
IV methylprednisolone hastens visual recovery in optic neuritis (ONTT)
Oral prednisolone monotherapy is harmful and not recommended
explanation.pearls: Aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing is added when bilateral, severe, or recurrent optic neuritis is present to identify neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder or MOG antibody-associated disease, which require different long-term immunotherapy.

sources.label: MSA: Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis and Management Guidelines
